titleStmt.title: Letter from Edward McHugh to Sir Robert Chalmers, 3 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Edward McHugh

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter to Robert Chalmers from Edward McHugh concerns the welfare of Elizabeth Traynor who resided at 20 Synnott Place Dublin. The letter refers to the helplessness of Elizabeth Traynor and her eight children in the aftermath of her husband's arrest and deportation without trial. The letter requests that the income tax authorities be asked to stop threatening to take her furniture. Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938), Under-Secretary for Ireland. After the Easter Rising of 1916 Chalmers was sent to Ireland as under-secretary, replacing Sir Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), who had resigned along with Augustine Birrell (1850-1933), his chief secretary. Edward McHugh was a Dublin solicitor and Elizabeth Traynor's husband, Thomas Traynor (1881-1921), was subsequently executed on 25 April 1921 during the Irish War of Independence.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary’s Office Registered papers, CSO-RP-1916-5615-11322

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Edward McHugh, Solicitor Offices-43 Rutland Square, Dublin, 3 July 1916 Dear Sir, Re Eliabeth Traynor 20 Synnott Place The husband of Mrs. Traynor (8 children) was
arrested and deported without trial.
She and the children are helpless except for
charity from the fund publicly subscribed.
The income tax authorities threaten to
take her furniture for 24/- 'income' tax.
Will you kindly have this stopped. Please reply. Yours faithfully, E. McHugh The Under Secretary Dublin Castle Reply that he should communicate
with the Board of Inland Revenue
Somerset House, London
